**5. Under the current legal framework, what is the consequence if a candidate fails to lodge an account of election expenses within the time and in the manner required by the law?**
A) The candidate is fined ₹1,00,000.
B) The candidate is disqualified for a period of three years from the date of the order.
C) The election of the candidate is automatically declared void.
D) The candidate is banned from joining any political party for five years.
**Answer: B.** Under Section 10A of RPA 1951, the ECI has the power to disqualify such a candidate for 3 years.
topics.oneLineRevision: Electoral reforms in India involve a mix of legislative amendments, technological shifts like VVPATs, and judicial interventions to curb the influence of money and crime, ensuring the sanctity of the "one person, one vote" principle.
topics.linkedTopics: Election Commission of India, RPA 1950 & 1951, Political Parties, Anti-Defection Law, Fundamental Rights (Article 19).

topics.teaser: High-yield revision topic for Emergency Provisions.
topics.whyThisMatters: This topic is the ultimate test of India’s **Federal Structure**. UPSC focuses on Emergency Provisions because they demonstrate how the Indian Constitution can transform from a "Federal" to a "Unitary" system without a formal amendment. Questions usually target the nuances of parliamentary approval, the impact on Fundamental Rights, and the safeguards introduced by the **44th Amendment Act (1978)** to prevent executive tyranny.
topics.coreConcept: Emergency Provisions are extraordinary measures contained in **Part XVIII** of the Constitution. They enable the Central Government to meet any abnormal situation (war, external aggression, armed rebellion, or financial instability) effectively. During an emergency, the federal distribution of powers is suspended, and the Union becomes all-powerful.
topics.title: Emergency Provisions
topics.upscGoldPoint: The suspension of **Articles 20 and 21** is prohibited even during a National Emergency. While Article 358 automatically suspends Article 19 (only in cases of War/External Aggression), Article 359 allows the President to suspend the right to move any court for the enforcement of other Fundamental Rights, **except** those guaranteed by Articles 20 (protection in respect of conviction for offences) and 21 (protection of life and personal liberty).
topics.deepUnderstanding:
There are three types of emergencies:

1.  **National Emergency (Article 352):** Declared on grounds of War, External Aggression, or **Armed Rebellion** (the word "Internal Disturbance" was replaced by the 44th Amendment). It requires a special majority in Parliament within one month. It can continue indefinitely with 6-month periodic approvals.
2.  **President’s Rule (Article 356):** Also known as State Emergency or Constitutional Emergency. Declared when a State government cannot function according to the Constitution. It requires a simple majority within two months. Maximum duration is 3 years.
3.  **Financial Emergency (Article 360):** Declared if the financial stability or credit of India is threatened. It requires a simple majority within two months. Once approved, it continues indefinitely until revoked (no periodic approval needed). **It has never been declared in India so far.**
topics.linkedFacts:
* **Written Advice:** The President can declare a National Emergency only after receiving a **written recommendation** from the Cabinet (not just the PM). This safeguard was added by the 44th Amendment.
* **Judicial Review:** The 38th Amendment (1975) made the President’s satisfaction immune to judicial review, but the **Minerva Mills case (1980)** restored the power of the courts to challenge an emergency declaration on grounds of malafide.
* **Article 355:** Imposes a duty on the Union to protect every State against external aggression and internal disturbance.
topics.trapZone:
* **Article 358 vs. 359:** 358 only deals with **Article 19** and is automatic. 359 deals with **other rights** and requires a specific Presidential Order. 358 applies to the whole of India; 359 can apply to the whole or any part of India.
* **Majority Requirement:** National Emergency (352) needs a **Special Majority** (2/3 present and voting + absolute majority). President’s Rule (356) and Financial Emergency (360) only need a **Simple Majority**.
* **Revocation:** A National Emergency must be revoked if the Lok Sabha passes a resolution disapproving it by a **Simple Majority**.
topics.memoryTrick:
**"A-S-S" (Approval, Status, Suspension):**
* **A**pproval: 1 month (Art 352), 2 months (Art 356/360).
* **S**pecial Majority: Only for National Emergency (352).
* **S**uspension of FRs: Only in National Emergency; never for Arts 20 & 21.
topics.prelimsSnapshot:
* **Art 352:** National (External/Internal).
* **Art 356:** State (Failure of machinery).
* **Art 360:** Financial.
* **Approval Period:** 1-2-2 (months).
* **44th Amendment:** The "Savior Amendment" that corrected Emergency abuses.
topics.mcqs:
**1. With reference to the National Emergency in India, consider the following statements:**
1. A proclamation of National Emergency may be applicable to the entire country or only a part of it.
2. The President can proclaim a National Emergency only after receiving a verbal advice from the Prime Minister.
3. Every resolution approving the proclamation must be passed by either House of Parliament by a special majority.
**Which of the statements given above is/are correct?**
A) 1 only
B) 1 and 3 only
C) 2 and 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: B.** Statement 2 is wrong; written advice from the **Cabinet** (not just PM) is mandatory under the 44th Amendment.

**2. Which of the following is/are the differences between Article 358 and Article 359?**
1. Article 358 is confined to Fundamental Rights under Article 19 only, whereas Article 359 extends to all those Fundamental Rights whose enforcement is suspended by the Presidential Order.
2. Article 358 automatically suspends the fundamental rights as soon as the emergency is declared, while Article 359 does not automatically suspend any Fundamental Right.
**Select the correct answer:**
A) 1 only
B) 2 only
C) Both 1 and 2
D) Neither 1 nor 2
**Answer: C.** Both statements correctly highlight the distinction between the automatic nature of Art 358 and the discretionary nature of Art 359.

**3. If the President’s Rule is imposed in a State under Article 356, what is the consequence?**
A) The State Legislative Assembly is automatically dissolved.
B) The Parliament assumes the power to make laws for that state.
C) The High Court of the State is suspended.
D) The Fundamental Rights of the citizens of that state are suspended.
**Answer: B.** The Assembly can be dissolved or kept in suspended animation, but it is not automatic. The Parliament gains legislative power, but the High Court's powers remain unaffected.

**4. Consider the following regarding a Financial Emergency under Article 360:**
1. There is no maximum period prescribed for its operation.
2. Repeated parliamentary approval is required every six months for its continuation.
3. The President can issue directions for the reduction of salaries of persons serving the Union and the States, including judges of the SC and HCs.
**Which of the statements given above are correct?**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 2 and 3 only
C) 1 and 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: C.** Statement 2 is incorrect; unlike Art 352 and 356, Financial Emergency does not require periodic parliamentary approval once it is passed.

**5. Under which of the following circumstances can the President's Rule be imposed in a state?**
1. If a state fails to comply with any direction from the Centre.
2. If no party is able to form a majority in the state assembly after elections.
3. If the state government is pursuing a policy that is anti-secular.
**Select the correct answer:**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 2 and 3 only
C) 1 and 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: D.** All three are valid grounds. Ground 1 is under Article 365, and Ground 3 was upheld by the SC in the S.R. Bommai case.
topics.oneLineRevision: Emergency provisions allow the Indian State to transition into a unitary command to tackle existential threats, provided that the executive remains accountable to the Parliament and the judiciary protects the non-suspendable rights of life and liberty.
topics.linkedTopics: Federal System, Fundamental Rights, 44th Amendment Act, S.R. Bommai Case, Center-State Relations.
Fiscal Federalism, GST Council, Article 275 (Grants-in-aid), State Finance Commission, Consolidated Fund of India, NITI Aayog.
topics.prelimsSnapshot:
* **First FC Chairman:** K.C. Neogy (1951).
* **15th FC Chairman:** N.K. Singh.
* **Shared Pool:** Includes all central taxes except cess and surcharges.
* **Eligibility:** Chairman should have experience in public affairs; members should be High Court judges (or qualified to be), or have specialized knowledge of finance, accounts, or economics.

---
topics.linkedFacts:
* **Article 281:** Requires the President to lay the FC report, along with an explanatory memorandum on action taken, before both Houses of Parliament.
* **Article 243-I & 243-Y:** State Finance Commissions are constituted by the Governor to review the financial position of Panchayats and Municipalities.
* **Composition:** Consists of a Chairman and four other members appointed by the President. Their qualifications are determined by **Parliament** (via the Finance Commission Act, 1951).
topics.mcqs:
**1. Consider the following statements regarding the Finance Commission:**
I. The qualifications for members are explicitly detailed under Article 280 of the Constitution.
II. The Commission can recommend the augmentation of the Consolidated Fund of a State to supplement the resources of the Panchayats.
**Which of the statements is/are correct?**
A) Only I
B) Only II
C) Both I and II
D) Neither I nor II
**Answer: B.** Article 280(3)(bb) empowers the FC to suggest measures for Panchayats; however, qualifications are determined by Parliament, not the Constitution itself.

**2. In the context of the 15th Finance Commission, which of the following criteria was assigned the highest weightage for horizontal devolution?**
A) Population (2011)
B) Area
C) Income Distance
D) Demographic Performance
**Answer: C.** Income Distance was assigned 45%, the highest among all criteria to ensure equity between rich and poor states.

**3. Which of the following is NOT a function of the Finance Commission?**
A) Distribution of net proceeds of taxes between Union and States.
B) Principles governing the grants-in-aid to the States.
C) Determining the salaries and allowances of the members of the Commission.
D) Any other matter referred to it by the President in the interest of sound finance.
**Answer: C.** Salaries and allowances are determined by the President as per the rules made by Parliament, not by the Commission itself.

**4. Assertion (A): The Finance Commission is a quasi-judicial body.**
**Reason (R): It has the powers of a civil court as per the Code of Civil Procedure 1908 while summoning witnesses.**
A) Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
B) Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation.
C) A is true, R is false.
D) A is false, R is true.
**Answer: A.** The Commission's power to summon and enforce attendance makes it quasi-judicial in its functioning.

**5. Which Article deals with the laying of the Finance Commission's report before the Parliament?**
A) Article 280
B) Article 281
C) Article 282
D) Article 263
**Answer: B.** Article 281 specifically governs the presentation of the report and the action-taken memorandum to Parliament.

---
topics.deepUnderstanding:
The Commission's primary mandate is to address two types of imbalances:
1.  **Vertical Imbalance:** The Center has more revenue-raising power, while States have more expenditure responsibilities (health, education, etc.).
2.  **Horizontal Imbalance:** Different states have different fiscal capacities and needs.

The Commission uses a "criteria-based formula" for horizontal distribution, which often includes factors like **Population (2011 census)**, **Area**, **Forest and Ecology**, **Income Distance**, and **Demographic Performance**.
topics.whyThisMatters: The Finance Commission (FC) is a cornerstone of **Fiscal Federalism** in India. For UPSC, it is a perennial favorite because it balances the vertical and horizontal fiscal imbalances between the Union and States. Questions frequently appear in Prelims regarding its constitutional provisions and in Mains (GS-II and GS-III) regarding cooperative federalism and revenue sharing formulas.
topics.memoryTrick:
**"A-280: Always 2-8-0 (To-Balance-Often)"**
Think of the FC as a "Balanced Scale"—it prevents the Center from becoming too heavy and the States from becoming too light.
topics.upscGoldPoint: The recommendations of the Finance Commission are **advisory in nature** and not binding on the Government. However, by convention, the Government usually accepts the recommendations relating to the sharing of tax proceeds.
topics.coreConcept: The Finance Commission is a **Quasi-Judicial, Constitutional body** established under **Article 280** of the Constitution. It is constituted by the President of India every five years (or earlier) to recommend the distribution of net proceeds of taxes between the Center and the States, and among the States themselves.
topics.title: Finance Commission
topics.teaser: High-yield revision topic for Finance Commission.
topics.trapZone:
* **The Trap:** Thinking the Qualifications of members are mentioned in the Constitution.
* **The Reality:** The Constitution authorizes **Parliament** to determine the qualifications.
* **The Trap:** Believing the FC decides the "Grants-in-aid" for all purposes.
* **The Reality:** It only recommends grants-in-aid to the revenues of States out of the **Consolidated Fund of India** under Article 275.
topics.oneLineRevision: The Finance Commission is a Presidentially-appointed constitutional body under Article 280 that acts as the "balancing wheel of fiscal federalism" by recommending the division of tax revenue between the Union and States.

topics.prelimsSnapshot:
* **Article:** 51A.
* **Part:** IV-A.
* **Amendment:** 42nd (1976) for first ten; 86th (2002) for the eleventh.
* **Committee:** Swaran Singh.
* **Status:** Non-justiciable; for citizens only.
topics.linkedTopics: Fundamental Rights, Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP), 42nd Amendment Act, 86th Amendment Act, Basic Structure Doctrine.
topics.whyThisMatters: Fundamental Duties (FDs) are a favorite for UPSC because they represent the "Moral Obligations" of a citizen, balancing the "Legal Claims" of Fundamental Rights. Questions often focus on distinguishing FDs from Directive Principles (DPSPs), their non-justiciable nature, and the specific amendments that added them. It is high-yield for both Prelims (identifying specific duties) and Mains (discussing the balance between rights and responsibilities).
topics.deepUnderstanding:
* **Origin:** Unlike Fundamental Rights, FDs were **not part of the original Constitution**. They were inspired by the Constitution of the erstwhile **USSR**.
* **Swaran Singh Committee (1976):** Recommended the inclusion of FDs during the Emergency. The Committee suggested 8 duties and even proposed penalties for non-compliance (which was rejected).
* **42nd Amendment Act (1976):** Added 10 Fundamental Duties by inserting a new Part IV-A and Article 51A.
* **86th Amendment Act (2002):** Added the **11th Fundamental Duty**—providing opportunities for education to children between 6 and 14 years.
* **Scope:** FDs are confined **only to citizens** and do not extend to foreigners.
* **Verma Committee (1999):** Identified the existence of legal provisions for the implementation of some FDs (e.g., Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act, Wildlife Protection Act).
topics.mcqs:
**1. Which of the following were recommendations of the Swaran Singh Committee that were NOT accepted by the government while incorporating Fundamental Duties?**
1. Parliament may provide for the imposition of such penalty or punishment as may be considered appropriate for non-compliance.
2. No law imposing such penalty shall be called in question in any court.
3. Duty to pay taxes should be a fundamental duty of the citizens.
**Select the correct answer:**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 2 and 3 only
C) 1 and 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: D.** All three were recommended by the committee but excluded by the Indira Gandhi government to keep the duties purely moral and non-punitive.

**2. Under the Constitution of India, which of the following is NOT a Fundamental Duty?**
A) To vote in public elections.
B) To develop the scientific temper.
C) To safeguard public property.
D) To abide by the Constitution and respect its ideals.
**Answer: A.** Voting is a Constitutional/Legal Right, not a Fundamental Duty under Article 51A.

**3. Consider the following statements regarding Fundamental Duties:**
1. They can be used by the courts in determining the constitutional validity of any law.
2. They can be enforced by writs like Mandamus if a citizen fails to perform them.
**Which of the statements given above is/are correct?**
A) 1 only
B) 2 only
C) Both 1 and 2
D) Neither 1 nor 2
**Answer: A.** Since FDs are non-justiciable, no writ can be issued for their enforcement. However, they serve as interpretive tools for the judiciary.

**4. The 11th Fundamental Duty, added by the 86th Constitutional Amendment Act (2002), is related to:**
A) Protecting and improving the natural environment.
B) Safeguarding public property and abjuring violence.
C) Providing opportunities for education to his child or ward between the age of 6 and 14 years.
D) Protecting monuments, places, and objects of artistic or historic interest.
**Answer: C.** This was added alongside Article 21A (Right to Education) to create a triadic responsibility (State, Parent, and Individual).

**5. With reference to the Fundamental Duties, which of the following is correct regarding their application?**
A) They apply to citizens, foreigners, and legal persons like corporations.
B) They apply only to citizens and not to foreigners.
C) They apply to all residents of India regardless of nationality.
D) They are enforceable against the State.
**Answer: B.** Fundamental Duties are exclusively for the citizens of India.
topics.linkedFacts:
* **Number of Duties:** Currently 11.
* **Nature:** They cover both **Civic Duties** (respecting the Flag) and **Ethical/Moral Duties** (cherishing noble ideals of the freedom struggle).
* **Japanese Precedent:** India is one of the few democratic constitutions in the world (besides Japan) that contains a list of duties for citizens.
* **Legal Enforcement:** While FDs themselves aren't enforceable, Parliament can provide for their imposition through specific legislation (e.g., Environment Protection Act).
topics.upscGoldPoint: Fundamental Duties are **non-justiciable**, meaning they cannot be enforced directly by the courts in case of violation. However, the Supreme Court has ruled that they can be used to determine the **constitutional validity** of any law. If a law seeks to give effect to a Fundamental Duty, it may be considered "reasonable" under Articles 14 or 19.
topics.memoryTrick:
**"S-S-S-S-H" (Selected Keywords):**
* **S**cientific temper (to develop).
* **S**overeignty (to uphold).
* **S**afeguard public property.
* **S**trive for excellence.
* **H**eritage (to value and preserve).
topics.title: Fundamental Duties
topics.coreConcept: Fundamental Duties are the statutory duties of the citizens of India, enshrined in **Part IV-A** of the Constitution. They serve as a constant reminder to citizens that while they enjoy rights, they also owe certain obligations to the nation and fellow citizens to maintain social harmony and progress.
topics.teaser: High-yield revision topic for Fundamental Duties.
topics.oneLineRevision: Fundamental Duties are a non-justiciable set of 11 moral and civic obligations added via the 42nd and 86th Amendments to remind citizens that the enjoyment of rights necessitates the performance of duties toward the nation.
topics.trapZone:
* **Enforceability:** Do not confuse FDs with Rights. You cannot go to court to ask someone to "be scientific" or "cherish ideals."
* **The "Tax" Trap:** The Swaran Singh Committee recommended that "Duty to pay taxes" be a Fundamental Duty, but this was **never accepted** or added to the Constitution.
* **Specific Wording:** UPSC often mixes phrases from DPSPs and FDs. For example, "to protect and improve the natural environment" is an FD (51A), but "to protect and improve the environment and safeguard forests" is also a DPSP (Art 48A).

topics.teaser: High-yield revision module for Fundamental Rights Overview.
topics.title: Fundamental Rights Overview
topics.coreConcept: Fundamental Rights (Part III, Articles 12-35) are the **Magna Carta of India**. They are "fundamental" because they are guaranteed and protected by the Constitution—the fundamental law of the land—and are essential for the material and moral development of individuals. Unlike ordinary legal rights, they are justiciable and protected directly by the Supreme Court.
topics.oneLineRevision: Fundamental Rights are justiciable guarantees in Part III that ensure individual liberty by limiting State power, subject to reasonable restrictions and judicial review.
topics.trapZone:
* **Rights vs. Citizenship:** Remember that some rights are available only to citizens (Arts. 15, 16, 19, 29, 30), while others are available to both citizens and foreigners (except enemy aliens).
* **Suspension vs. Termination:** Fundamental Rights are suspended, not permanently deleted during an emergency. Art. 19 is automatically suspended only in cases of War/External Aggression, not Armed Rebellion.
* **The "Property" Trap:** The Right to Property is no longer a Fundamental Right (removed by 44th Amendment, 1978); it is now a legal right under Art. 300-A.
topics.prelimsSnapshot:
* **Part:** III 
* **Articles:** 12 to 35
* **Nature:** Justiciable (Art. 32 allows direct move to SC).
* **Six Categories:** 1. Right to Equality (14-18)
  2. Right to Freedom (19-22)
  3. Right against Exploitation (23-24)
  4. Right to Freedom of Religion (25-28)
  5. Cultural and Educational Rights (29-30)
  6. Right to Constitutional Remedies (32)
topics.linkedTopics: Directive Principles of State Policy (DPSP), Fundamental Duties, Basic Structure Doctrine, Preamble, Writ Jurisdiction.
topics.upscGoldPoint: Fundamental Rights are **not absolute but qualified**. The State can impose "reasonable restrictions" on them. Whether a restriction is "reasonable" or not is decided by the courts, not the Parliament, ensuring a balance between individual liberty and social control.
topics.memoryTrick: Think of FRs as a **"Protective Shield"** against the "Sword of the State." If the State (Art. 12) swings the sword (a law/action), the Shield (Art. 13 + Judiciary) blocks it to protect the individual.
topics.whyThisMatters: This is arguably the most critical topic in the UPSC syllabus. Since 2011, almost every Prelims paper has featured 2-4 questions from this section. It forms the bedrock of **Constitutionalism** and **Limited Government**, and for the Mains (GS Paper II), it is indispensable for discussing judicial activism, social justice, and the evolving nature of Indian democracy.
topics.deepUnderstanding:
The philosophy of Fundamental Rights is to establish **"a government of laws and not of men."** They act as limitations on the tyranny of the executive and arbitrary laws of the legislature. 
* **Negative vs. Positive:** Some are negative in character (placing limitations on the State, e.g., Art. 14), while others are positive (conferring privileges on persons, e.g., Art. 25).
* **Suspension:** They can be suspended during a National Emergency (Art. 352), **except** for the rights guaranteed by Articles 20 and 21.
* **Amendability:** They are not sacrosanct; Parliament can curtail them via Constitutional Amendment (Art. 368), provided the "Basic Structure" of the Constitution remains intact (Kesavananda Bharati Case, 1973).
topics.linkedFacts:
* **Inspiration:** Borrowed from the **Bill of Rights** of the US Constitution.
* **Article 12:** Defines "State" to include Government and Parliament, State legislatures, local authorities, and statutory bodies like LIC or ONGC.
* **Article 13:** Declares that all laws that are inconsistent with or in derogation of any of the fundamental rights shall be void. This provides the basis for **Judicial Review**.
topics.mcqs:
**1. Which of the following is NOT correctly matched regarding the availability of Fundamental Rights to citizens and foreigners?**
A. Art 15: Citizens only
B. Art 19: Citizens only
C. Art 21: Citizens and Foreigners
D. Art 29: Citizens and Foreigners
**Answer: D** (Art. 29 and 30 are available only to citizens/minorities of India).

**2. With reference to Article 13 of the Indian Constitution, consider the following statements:**
1. The term 'law' includes ornaments, customs, or usages having the force of law.
2. A Constitutional Amendment is not a law and hence cannot be challenged.
**Which of the statements given above is/are correct?**
A. 1 only
B. 2 only
C. Both 1 and 2
D. Neither 1 nor 2
**Answer: A** (Per the Kesavananda Bharati case, even a Constitutional Amendment can be challenged if it violates the Basic Structure).

**3. The 'Writ of Mandamus' cannot be issued against which of the following?**
1. A private individual or body.
2. The President of India or State Governors.
3. A Chief Justice of a High Court acting in a judicial capacity.
**Select the correct answer using the code below:**
A. 1 and 2 only
B. 2 and 3 only
C. 1 and 3 only
D. 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: D** (Mandamus is issued to public officials; it has specific exemptions for the heads of state and judicial functions).

**4. Which Amendment Act deleted the Right to Property from the list of Fundamental Rights?**
A. 42nd Amendment Act
B. 44th Amendment Act
C. 52nd Amendment Act
D. 24th Amendment Act
**Answer: B**

**5. Under the Indian Constitution, the "Right to Equality" does NOT include:**
A. Equality before law
B. Social equality
C. Economic equality
D. Equality of opportunity
**Answer: C** (The Constitution provides for political and social equality in FRs; economic equality is a directive principle/aspiration).

topics.coreConcept: The Governor is the chief executive head of a state. Unlike the President of India, who is elected, the Governor is **appointed by the President** and serves as a vital link between the Union Government and the State Government. While the Governor is a nominal executive, the Constitution grants them certain **explicit discretionary powers** that the President does not possess.
topics.title: The Governor (Articles 153–167)
topics.teaser: High-yield revision topic for Governor.
topics.whyThisMatters: The Governor’s role is one of the most controversial and frequently tested topics in UPSC. As a "Dual Entity"—the **Constitutional Head of the State** and a **Representative of the Centre**—the Governor sits at the heart of Center-State relations. Questions usually focus on discretionary powers, the friction between the Governor and the Chief Minister, and recommendations from commissions like Sarkaria and Punchhi.
topics.upscGoldPoint: While the President’s "aid and advice" from the Council of Ministers is virtually binding (after one reconsideration), **Article 163** states that if any question arises whether a matter falls within the Governor's discretion, the **decision of the Governor is final**. The validity of anything done by the Governor cannot be called into question on the ground that they ought or ought not to have acted in their discretion.
topics.deepUnderstanding:
* **Appointment & Tenure:** Appointed by the President by warrant under his hand and seal. The Governor holds office during the **pleasure of the President**. There is no fixed tenure or security of term, and the grounds for removal are not mentioned in the Constitution.
* **Dual Role:**
    1. **State Executive:** Acts on the advice of the State Council of Ministers (CoM).
    2. **Agent of the Centre:** Reports to the President, especially regarding the failure of constitutional machinery (Article 356).
* **Discretionary Powers:**
    * **Constitutional Discretion:** Reserving a bill for the President’s consideration (Article 200); recommending President's Rule; exercising functions as the administrator of an adjoining UT.
    * **Situational Discretion:** Appointing a CM when no party has a clear majority; dismissal of the CoM when it cannot prove majority; dissolution of the Assembly if the CoM has lost majority.
* **Veto Power over State Bills:** The Governor can give assent, withhold assent, return the bill (if not a Money Bill), or **reserve the bill for the President**. Once reserved, the Governor has no further role; the President decides.
topics.linkedFacts:
* **7th Amendment Act (1956):** Facilitated the appointment of the **same person** as Governor for two or more states.
* **Qualifications:** Must be a citizen of India and have completed **35 years** of age.
* **Pardoning Power (Article 161):** Can grant pardons, reprieves, etc., for state laws. However, the Governor **cannot** pardon a death sentence (only the President can) or commute a sentence by a Court Martial.
* **Immunity:** Enjoys personal immunity from legal liability for official acts. No criminal proceedings can be instituted during the term of office.
topics.trapZone:
* **Removal:** The Governor can be removed at any time by the President. There is **no impeachment** process for the Governor, unlike the President or Judges.
* **Money Bills:** Like the President, the Governor cannot return a Money Bill for reconsideration.
* **Appointment of Judges:** The Governor is **consulted** by the President for HC judge appointments, but the Governor **appoints** the District Judges (Art 233).
* **Discretionary Difference:** The Constitution envisages the possibility of the Governor acting at times **without** advice (Art 163), whereas no such explicit provision exists for the President.
topics.memoryTrick:
**"P-A-L-S" (Constitutional Status):**
* **P**leasure of the President (Tenure).
* **A**gent of the Centre.
* **L**ink between Union and State.
* **S**tate Executive Head.
topics.prelimsSnapshot:
* **Article 153:** Governor for each state.
* **Article 154:** Executive power of state vested in Governor.
* **Article 161:** Pardoning powers (limited).
* **Article 200:** Assent to bills (Reservation power).
* **Article 213:** Ordinance-making power.
topics.mcqs:
**1. Consider the following statements regarding the office of the Governor:**
1. The Constitution lays down that a person cannot be appointed as Governor of their home state.
2. The Governor can be removed by the President only on grounds of misbehavior or incapacity.
3. The oath of the Governor is administered by the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court.
**Which of the statements given above is/are correct?**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 3 only
C) 1 and 3 only
D) 2 only
**Answer: B.** Statement 1 is a **convention**, not a constitutional rule. Statement 2 is incorrect because the Constitution provides **no grounds** for the Governor's removal.

**2. With reference to the legislative powers of the Governor, which of the following is correct?**
A) The Governor can nominate 1/6th of the members of the State Legislative Assembly.
B) The Governor must reserve all bills passed by the State Legislature for the President's consideration.
C) The Governor can promulgate an ordinance only when both Houses of the State Legislature (if bicameral) are not in session.
D) An ordinance issued by the Governor has the same force as an act of the state legislature.
**Answer: D.** Ordinances have equal legal weight. Choice A is wrong (1/6th is for Legislative Council); Choice B is wrong (only specific bills like those endangering HC status must be reserved); Choice C is wrong (it can be issued if even one house is not in session).

**3. Which of the following discretionary powers are available to the Governor of a state?**
1. Sending a report to the President for imposing President's Rule.
2. Appointing the ministers.
3. Reserving certain bills passed by the State Legislature for the consideration of the President.
4. Making the rules to conduct the business of the state government.
**Select the correct answer:**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 1 and 3 only
C) 2, 3, and 4 only
D) 1, 2, 3, and 4
**Answer: B.** Appointing ministers (2) and making rules of business (4) are executive actions done on the advice of the CM/CoM, not discretionary.

**4. How does the pardoning power of the Governor differ from that of the President?**
A) The Governor can pardon death sentences in cases involving state law.
B) The Governor cannot pardon a death sentence even if a state law prescribes it.
C) The Governor has no power to grant reprieves or respites.
D) The President's power extends to state laws, whereas the Governor's power extends to central laws.
**Answer: B.** The President is the **only** authority to pardon a death sentence. The Governor can only suspend, remit, or commute a death sentence.

**5. Under Article 200, when a bill is reserved by the Governor for the consideration of the President:**
A) The President must give his assent within 6 months.
B) The President may return the bill for reconsideration to the State House.
C) If the bill is passed again by the State Legislature, the President is bound to give assent.
D) The Governor can withdraw the bill from the President if the State Government changes.
**Answer: B.** Unlike a central bill, the President is **not bound** to give assent to a state bill even if it is passed a second time by the State Legislature.
topics.oneLineRevision: The Governor is an appointed constitutional head who acts as the primary bridge between the Union and the States, possessing unique discretionary powers to ensure the state government functions in accordance with the Constitution.
topics.linkedTopics: President, Chief Minister and Council of Ministers, Center-State Relations, Sarkaria Commission, President's Rule (Article 356), State Legislature.

topics.teaser: High-yield revision topic for High Courts & Subordinate Courts.
topics.whyThisMatters: The Indian Judiciary is an **integrated system**, and while the Supreme Court is the apex, the High Courts (HCs) and Subordinate Courts are where the vast majority of litigation occurs. UPSC focuses on this topic to test the "Federal" versus "Unitary" nature of our judiciary, the unique **Writ Jurisdiction** of HCs (which is wider than the SC's), and the administrative control HCs exercise over the lower judiciary.
topics.title: High Courts & Subordinate Courts
topics.coreConcept: The High Court stands at the head of a state's judicial administration. Below it lies the hierarchy of Subordinate Courts (District Courts and lower). Unlike the US, where there are separate federal and state courts, India has a single integrated hierarchy that enforces both Central and State laws.
topics.upscGoldPoint: Under **Article 226**, the Writ Jurisdiction of a High Court is **wider** than that of the Supreme Court under Article 32. While the SC can issue writs only for the enforcement of **Fundamental Rights**, a High Court can issue them for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights **as well as for any other ordinary legal right**.
topics.deepUnderstanding:
* **High Court Establishment:** The Parliament can establish a common High Court for two or more states or for two or more states and a union territory (e.g., Punjab and Haryana HC).
* **Appointment:** Judges of the HC are appointed by the **President** (not the Governor) after consultation with the Chief Justice of India, the Governor of the State, and the Chief Justice of the HC.
* **Independence:** HC judges are removed by the President on the same grounds and in the same manner as an SC judge (Impeachment). Their salaries are charged on the **Consolidated Fund of the State**, but their pensions are charged on the **Consolidated Fund of India**.
* **Subordinate Courts (Articles 233–237):**
    * **District Judge:** The highest judicial authority in the district. Appointed by the **Governor** in consultation with the High Court.
    * **Control:** The High Court has administrative control over district courts and other subordinate courts (postings, leave, promotion, and discipline).
topics.linkedFacts:
* **First High Courts:** Established in **1862** in Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras.
* **Total High Courts:** Currently **25** in India.
* **Age of Retirement:** 62 years for HC judges (compared to 65 for SC judges).
* **Transfer:** The President can transfer a judge from one HC to another after consulting the CJI.
topics.trapZone:
* **Removal Power:** A High Court judge is appointed by the President and can only be **removed by the President**, not the Governor.
* **Pension Source:** This is a classic UPSC trap—the **salary** of an HC judge comes from the **State**, but the **pension** comes from the **Centre (Consolidated Fund of India)**.
* **Jurisdiction:** While an HC has wider writ jurisdiction (Art 226), the SC has wider **Territorial Jurisdiction** because its writs apply throughout India, whereas an HC's writ usually applies within its state boundaries.
topics.memoryTrick:
**"S-A-P" (Subordinate Court Appointment):**
* **S**ubordinate Court Judges (other than District Judges) are appointed by the...
* **A**uthority of the **Governor**...
* **P**ost consultation with the State **Public Service Commission** and the **High Court**.
topics.prelimsSnapshot:
* **Article 214:** Each state shall have a High Court.
* **Article 215:** HC to be a Court of Record.
* **Article 226:** Writ Jurisdiction.
* **Article 227:** Power of superintendence over all courts within its jurisdiction.
* **Article 233:** Appointment of District Judges.
topics.mcqs:
**1. Consider the following statements regarding the High Courts in India:**
1. The Parliament can extend the jurisdiction of a High Court to any Union Territory.
2. The expenses of the High Court, including salaries and pensions of judges, are charged on the Consolidated Fund of the State.
3. A High Court can withdraw a case pending in a subordinate court if it involves a substantial question of law.
**Which of the statements given above is/are correct?**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 1 and 3 only
C) 2 and 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: B.** Statement 2 is incorrect because the **pensions** of High Court judges are charged on the Consolidated Fund of India, not the State.

**2. Which of the following is the correct requirement for a person to be appointed as a Judge of a High Court?**
A) He must have held a judicial office in the territory of India for ten years.
B) He must have been an advocate of a High Court for five years.
C) He must be, in the opinion of the President, a distinguished jurist.
D) He must have reached the age of 35 years.
**Answer: A.** Unlike the Supreme Court, the "Distinguished Jurist" category does **not** exist for High Court appointments. The advocacy requirement is 10 years, not 5.

**3. The power of 'Judicial Review' of the High Court means the power to:**
A) Review its own judgments.
B) Examine the constitutionality of both legislative enactments and executive orders of both Central and State governments.
C) Supervise the functioning of subordinate courts.
D) Advise the Governor on legal matters.
**Answer: B.** High Courts have the power to declare any law or order unconstitutional if it violates the Constitution.

**4. With reference to the 'District Judge', which of the following statements is correct?**
A) He possesses only civil powers, while criminal powers are with the Sessions Judge.
B) He is appointed by the President of India.
C) When he hears criminal cases, he is called the Sessions Judge.
D) He cannot award capital punishment (death sentence).
**Answer: C.** The same person acts as District Judge (civil) and Sessions Judge (criminal). He **can** award death sentences, but they must be confirmed by the High Court.

**5. Under Article 227 of the Indian Constitution, the High Court has the power of 'Superintendence'. This includes:**
1. Calling for returns from subordinate courts.
2. Issuing general rules and prescribing forms for regulating the practice and proceedings of such courts.
3. Power of superintendence over tribunals as well as courts.
**Select the correct answer:**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 2 and 3 only
C) 1 and 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: D.** Article 227 is very broad and covers both administrative and judicial superintendence over all courts and tribunals (except military tribunals) within its territorial jurisdiction.
topics.oneLineRevision: The High Courts and Subordinate Courts form a robust, integrated judicial ladder where the HC acts as a protector of both fundamental and legal rights through its expansive writ jurisdiction and administrative oversight of the lower judiciary.
topics.linkedTopics: Supreme Court of India, Writ Jurisdiction (Article 32 vs 226), Separation of Powers, Judicial Activism, Lok Adalats, Gram Nyayalayas.

topics.title: Historical Background
topics.coreConcept:
The Indian Constitution did not emerge in a vacuum; it is a result of **gradual institutional evolution**. This process is divided into two distinct phases:
1.  **The Company Rule (1773–1858):** Efforts by the British Parliament to regulate the East India Company.
2.  **The Crown Rule (1858–1947):** Direct administration by the British Monarch, leading to increasing Indian participation and eventual sovereignty.
topics.whyThisMatters: This topic is the bedrock of **Indian Polity** for the UPSC. It bridges History and Constitution, helping aspirants understand why certain provisions (like the Bicameral legislature or the federal structure) exist today. It is high-yield because 1–2 questions in Prelims are almost guaranteed every year, often focusing on the subtle shifts from "Company Rule" to "Crown Rule."
topics.teaser: High-yield revision topic for Historical Background.
topics.upscGoldPoint: The **Government of India Act of 1935** is the "Blueprint" of the current Constitution. About **60-70%** of our constitutional framework—including the Federal Scheme, Office of Governor, Judiciary, and Public Service Commissions—is derived directly from this Act.
topics.deepUnderstanding:
The evolution reflects a journey from **Centralization to Decentralization**:

* **Centralization Peak:** The **Charter Act of 1833** made the Governor-General of Bengal the "Governor-General of India," stripping Bombay and Madras of legislative powers.
* **Decentralization Reset:** The **Indian Councils Act of 1861** reversed this trend, restoring legislative powers to provinces and initiating the "Representative" era.
* **The Communal Wedge:** The **Morley-Minto Reforms (1909)** introduced a separate electorate for Muslims, legally institutionalizing communalism in Indian politics.
* **The Federal Experiment:** The **1919 Act** introduced **Dyarchy** (dual government) at the provinces, while the **1935 Act** attempted to introduce it at the Centre (though never fully implemented).
topics.linkedFacts:
* **First Governor-General of Bengal:** Warren Hastings (1773).
* **First Governor-General of India:** Lord William Bentinck (1833).
* **First Viceroy of India:** Lord Canning (1858).
* **Law Commission:** First established under the Charter Act of 1833 with Lord Macaulay.
* **Satyendra Prasad Sinha:** First Indian to join the Viceroy’s Executive Council (1909).
topics.trapZone:
* **Dyarchy vs. Bicameralism:** Don't confuse them. Dyarchy (dual rule) was introduced in provinces in 1919. Bicameralism (two houses) was introduced at the Centre in 1919 and in provinces in 1935.
* **Portfolio System:** Often mistakenly attributed to 1858; it was actually given statutory recognition by the **Indian Councils Act of 1861**.
* **Supreme Court:** The SC at Calcutta (1774) was a colonial court under the 1773 Act. The **Federal Court** (precursor to our current SC) was established by the 1935 Act.
topics.memoryTrick:
**"E-I-E-I-O" for the 1833 Act:**
* **E**nd of Tea/China monopoly.
* **I**ndia gets a Governor-General.
* **E**uropean colonization legalized.
* **I**ntroduction of Law Commission attempt.
* **O**fficial end of Company as a commercial body.
topics.prelimsSnapshot:
* **1773:** Foundation of central administration.
* **1784:** Distinction between Commercial and Political functions (Double Government).
* **1813:** Ended Company’s monopoly (except Tea and China trade); ₹1 Lakh for education.
* **1853:** Separation of Legislative and Executive functions; Open competition for Civil Services.
* **1919:** Introduced "Transferred" and "Reserved" subjects.
topics.mcqs:
**1. Which of the following Acts for the first time made it possible for Indians to take some share in the administration of their country, specifically mentioning that no Indian should be debarred from holding any office by reason of religion, place of birth, or color?**
A) Charter Act of 1813
B) Charter Act of 1833
C) Indian Councils Act 1861
D) Government of India Act 1858
**Answer: B.** Section 87 of the 1833 Act laid this down, though it remained a "paper pledge" due to opposition from the Court of Directors.

**2. Consider the following statements regarding the Morley-Minto Reforms of 1909:**
1. It increased the size of legislative councils at both Central and Provincial levels.
2. It allowed for the first time the association of Indians with the Executive Councils.
3. It introduced a system of direct elections for the Imperial Legislative Council.
**Which of the statements given above is/are correct?**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 2 and 3 only
C) 1 and 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: A.** The 1909 Act retained an official majority in the Central Council and used indirect elections; direct elections were introduced later in 1919.

**3. The principle of 'Dyarchy' introduced under the Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms involved:**
A) Division of the Central Legislature into two houses.
B) Double government at the Centre and the Provinces.
C) Division of subjects delegated to the provinces into two parts.
D) Introduction of a dual system of control by the British Parliament and the EIC.
**Answer: C.** It divided provincial subjects into 'Transferred' (administered by Ministers) and 'Reserved' (administered by the Governor).

**4. The Government of India Act of 1935 provided for:**
1. The establishment of an All-India Federation.
2. The adoption of Dyarchy at the Centre.
3. The abolition of Dyarchy in the provinces.
4. The establishment of a Federal Court.
**Select the correct answer:**
A) 1, 2, and 3 only
B) 2, 3, and 4 only
C) 1 and 4 only
D) 1, 2, 3, and 4
**Answer: D.** All four are key features of the 1935 Act, though the Federation never came into being because princely states refused to join.

**5. Which Act first introduced the "Representative Element" by providing for the nomination of non-official members to the legislative councils?**
A) Indian Councils Act, 1861
B) Indian Councils Act, 1892
C) Government of India Act, 1858
D) Charter Act, 1853
**Answer: A.** Lord Canning nominated three Indians (Raja of Benares, Maharaja of Patiala, and Sir Dinkar Rao) to his legislative council in 1862.
topics.oneLineRevision: The Indian Constitution is an evolutionary outcome of British legislative acts that transitioned India from a mercantile company’s fiefdom to a decentralized, representative, and eventually sovereign federal democracy.
topics.linkedTopics: Making of the Constitution, Preamble, Federal System in India, Parliamentary vs. Presidential System, Emergency Provisions.

topics.title: Inter-State Relations
topics.teaser: UPSC focuses on this topic because it covers the mechanisms for dispute resolution (like river water disputes) and cooperation (like the Inter-State Council).
topics.whyThisMatters: The success of Indian federalism depends not just on how the Centre treats the States, but on how States interact with each other. UPSC focuses on this topic because it covers the **mechanisms for dispute resolution** (like river water disputes) and **cooperation** (like the Inter-State Council). It is high-yield for Prelims due to the technical differences between various councils and for Mains in the context of "Cooperative and Competitive Federalism."
topics.coreConcept: Inter-State relations involve the constitutional and statutory provisions that facilitate harmony and resolve conflicts between the constituent units of the Indian Union. The Constitution aims to promote "comity" (mutual courtesy) through four main channels: **Adjudication of water disputes**, **Coordination through councils**, **Mutual recognition of public acts**, and **Freedom of inter-state trade**.
topics.upscGoldPoint: The **Inter-State Council (Article 263)** is a **recommendatory** body. Even though it is chaired by the Prime Minister and includes all Chief Ministers, its decisions are not legally binding on the governments. It is an "extra-judicial" mechanism for settling disputes and discussing common policies.
topics.deepUnderstanding:
There are four pillars of Inter-State relations:

1.  **Inter-State Water Disputes (Article 262):**
    * Parliament may by law provide for the adjudication of any dispute relating to the use, distribution, or control of waters of any inter-state river or river valley.
    * Parliament can **exclude the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court** and all other courts in such disputes.
    * Under this, the Inter-State River Water Disputes Act (1956) was enacted, allowing the Centre to set up ad hoc tribunals.

2.  **Inter-State Council (Article 263):**
    * Established by the President (first in 1990 on Sarkaria Commission's advice).
    * **Composition:** PM (Chairman), CMs of all States/UTs with assemblies, Administrators of other UTs, and six Union Cabinet ministers nominated by the PM.
    

3.  **Public Acts, Records and Judicial Proceedings (Article 261):**
    * "Full faith and credit" is to be given throughout India to public acts, records, and judicial proceedings of the Union and of every State. This ensures that a judgment of a civil court in one state is executable in another.

4.  **Inter-State Trade and Commerce (Articles 301–307):**
    * Article 301 declares that trade, commerce, and intercourse throughout the territory of India shall be free. However, Parliament can impose "reasonable restrictions" in the public interest.

5.  **Zonal Councils (Statutory):**
    * Established by the **States Reorganisation Act (1956)**, not the Constitution.
    * Five zones (North, South, East, West, Central). A sixth, the **North-Eastern Council**, was created by a separate Act in 1971.
    * **Chairman:** The Union Home Minister is the common chairman for all Zonal Councils.
topics.linkedFacts:
* **Article 131:** The Supreme Court has **original jurisdiction** in inter-state disputes (except water disputes excluded under Art 262).
* **Sarkaria Commission:** Strongly recommended that the Inter-State Council be made a permanent body.
* **Standing Committee of ISC:** Chaired by the Union Home Minister to process matters for the Council's consideration.
topics.trapZone:
* **Constitutional vs. Statutory:** The Inter-State Council (Art 263) is **Constitutional**. The Zonal Councils are **Statutory**. This is a classic "statement-based" trap in Prelims.
* **Water Disputes:** While the SC has original jurisdiction in most inter-state matters (Art 131), it is **barred** from inter-state river water disputes if Parliament so provides under Art 262.
* **Zonal Council Chair:** The Chairman is the **Union Home Minister**, not the Prime Minister (who chairs the Inter-State Council).
topics.memoryTrick:
**"W-I-Z" (Inter-State Mechanisms):**
* **W**ater Tribunals (Art 262).
* **I**nter-State Council (Art 263).
* **Z**onal Councils (Statutory).
*(Think: These mechanisms make the State-State relationship "Whiz" along smoothly.)*
topics.prelimsSnapshot:
* **Art 261:** Full Faith and Credit.
* **Art 262:** River Water Disputes (Parliamentary law).
* **Art 263:** Inter-State Council (Presidential order).
* **Art 301:** Freedom of Trade and Commerce.
* **Zonal Councils:** Statutory (Home Minister as Chair).
* **River Tribunals:** Decisions have the force of an SC decree.
topics.mcqs:
**1. Consider the following statements regarding the Inter-State Council:**
1. It is a permanent constitutional body established by the President of India.
2. The Prime Minister acts as the Chairman, while the Union Home Minister acts as the Vice-Chairman.
3. Its duties include investigating and discussing subjects in which the States and the Union have a common interest.
**Which of the statements given above is/are correct?**
A) 3 only
B) 1 and 2 only
C) 1 and 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: A.** Statement 1 is false (it is not permanent; the President can establish it "at any time"). Statement 2 is false (there is no Vice-Chairman; the Home Minister chairs the **Standing Committee**).

**2. With reference to the Zonal Councils, which of the following is correct?**
A) They are constitutional bodies under Article 263.
B) They are chaired by the Prime Minister of India.
C) They were established by the States Reorganisation Act, 1956.
D) Their decisions are binding on the constituent states.
**Answer: C.** Zonal Councils are statutory bodies aimed at promoting regional cooperation.

**3. Under Article 262, the Parliament has the power to exclude the jurisdiction of which of the following in respect of inter-state river water disputes?**
A) The High Courts only.
B) The Supreme Court only.
C) Both the Supreme Court and all other courts.
D) The Supreme Court, but only with the consent of the affected states.
**Answer: C.** The Constitution allows Parliament to completely insulate water dispute adjudication from the regular judiciary to ensure technical/tribunal-based resolution.

**4. The "Full Faith and Credit" clause under Article 261 of the Indian Constitution applies to:**
1. Public acts of the Union and the States.
2. Judicial proceedings of the Union and the States.
3. Criminal judgments of a court in one state being executable in another.
**Select the correct answer:**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 2 and 3 only
C) 1 and 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: A.** The clause applies only to **civil** judgments; criminal laws and judgments of one state are not executable in another in the same manner.

**5. Which of the following is the primary objective of the Inter-State Council as per Article 263?**
A) To collect taxes and distribute them among states.
B) To act as a court of law for inter-state boundary disputes.
C) Coordination between the States and between the Union and States.
D) To prepare the development plans for the Zonal Councils.
**Answer: C.** It is fundamentally a coordination and policy-discussion forum.
topics.oneLineRevision: Inter-State relations provide a constitutional and statutory framework for resolving river water conflicts and fostering administrative and regional coordination through councils, ensuring that federal units work in synergy.
topics.linkedTopics: Federal System, Center-State Relations, States Reorganisation Act 1956, Cooperative Federalism, Seventh Schedule.

topics.coreConcept: The Constitution was not written by a single individual but was drafted by the **Constituent Assembly**, a body of indirectly elected representatives. It functioned both as a **constituent body** (to frame the Constitution) and a **legislative body** (to enact ordinary laws) between 1946 and 1950.
topics.title: Making of the Constitution
topics.whyThisMatters: This topic is the "Genesis" of the Indian Republic. UPSC frequently tests the specific dates, committees, and the philosophical shifts that occurred during the Constituent Assembly debates. Understanding the "how" and "who" behind the Constitution is essential for answering questions on Constitutional Morality and the intent of the framers in both Prelims and Mains (GS Paper II).
topics.teaser: High-yield revision topic for Making of the Constitution.
topics.upscGoldPoint: The **Objectives Resolution**, moved by Jawaharlal Nehru on **December 13, 1946**, is the most critical document of this phase. It laid down the fundamental ideals and philosophy of the Constitution and later became the basis for the **Preamble**.
topics.deepUnderstanding:
The making of the Constitution followed a structured, democratic process:

* **Genesis:** The idea was first proposed by **M.N. Roy** in 1934. It was officially demanded by the INC in 1935 and accepted in principle by the British in the **"August Offer" of 1940**.
* **Composition:** The Assembly was constituted in November 1946 under the **Cabinet Mission Plan**. It was a partly elected and partly nominated body. Members were elected by the provincial assemblies via proportional representation.
* **Working:** The Assembly held its first meeting on **December 9, 1946**. Dr. Sachchidanand Sinha was the temporary President, later replaced by **Dr. Rajendra Prasad**. 
* **The Drafting Committee:** Established on August 29, 1947, chaired by **Dr. B.R. Ambedkar**. This 7-member committee was responsible for scrutinizing the draft prepared by the Constitutional Advisor, **Sir B.N. Rau**.
* **Enactment:** It took **2 years, 11 months, and 18 days** to complete. The Constitution was adopted on **November 26, 1949**, and came into full force on **January 26, 1950** (to commemorate the Purna Swaraj declaration of 1930).
topics.linkedFacts:
* **Elephant:** Adopted as the symbol (seal) of the Constituent Assembly.
* **Calligrapher:** Prem Behari Narain Raizada wrote the original Constitution in a flowing italic style.
* **Artist:** Nandalal Bose and Beohar Rammanohar Sinha decorated and illuminated the manuscript.
* **Secretary:** H.V.R. Iyengar was the Secretary to the Constituent Assembly.
* **Chief Draftsman:** S.N. Mukherjee was the Chief Draftsman of the Constitution in the Assembly.
topics.trapZone:
* **Sovereignty:** Note that the Constituent Assembly became a fully sovereign body only after the **Indian Independence Act of 1947**, not at its inception in 1946.
* **Status of the Preamble:** The Preamble was enacted **after** the entire Constitution was already enacted to ensure it was in tune with the provisions.
* **B.N. Rau:** He was the **Constitutional Advisor**, but he was **not a member** of the Constituent Assembly.
* **Membership:** Mahatma Gandhi and Muhammad Ali Jinnah were notably **not members** of the Constituent Assembly.
topics.memoryTrick:
**Drafting Committee Members (B.R. Ambedkar + 6 others):**
* **"Ambedkar** and **Madhav** saw **Khaitan** in **Munshi's** **Ayyar** and **Ayyangar** garden with **Saadullah**."
*(Ambedkar, N. Madhava Rau, D.P. Khaitan, K.M. Munshi, Alladi Krishnaswamy Ayyar, N. Gopalaswamy Ayyangar, Syed Mohammad Saadullah).*
topics.prelimsSnapshot:
* **Dec 9, 1946:** First meeting (boycotted by Muslim League).
* **Dec 13, 1946:** Objectives Resolution moved.
* **July 22, 1947:** Adoption of the National Flag.
* **Nov 26, 1949:** Constitution adopted (contained Preamble, 395 Articles, 8 Schedules).
* **Jan 24, 1950:** Final session; National Anthem and National Song adopted; Dr. Rajendra Prasad elected first President.
topics.mcqs:
**1. With reference to the Constituent Assembly of India, consider the following statements:**
1. The seats were allotted to each Province and Princely State in proportion to their respective population.
2. The representatives of the Princely States were to be elected by the people of those states through direct suffrage.
3. The Assembly included representatives from all sections of Indian society, including women.
**Which of the statements given above is/are correct?**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 1 and 3 only
C) 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: B.** Statement 2 is incorrect because representatives of Princely States were **nominated** by the heads of the states, not elected.

**2. Which of the following functions were performed by the Constituent Assembly of India?**
1. It ratified India's membership of the Commonwealth in 1949.
2. It adopted the National Anthem and National Song on January 24, 1950.
3. It acted as the Provisional Parliament of India from January 26, 1950, until the first general elections.
**Select the correct answer:**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 2 and 3 only
C) 1 and 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: D.** All statements are correct. The Assembly had a dual role and continued as the Provisional Parliament until 1952.

**3. The 'Objectives Resolution' passed by the Constituent Assembly contained which of the following features?**
1. It declared India as an Independent Sovereign Republic.
2. It provided for a federal form of government with residuary powers vesting in the Centre.
3. It guaranteed justice, equality, and freedom to all people of India.
**Select the correct answer:**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 1 and 3 only
C) 2 and 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: B.** Statement 2 is incorrect; interestingly, the Objectives Resolution originally stated that **residuary powers shall vest in the units** (states), though the final Constitution changed this to the Centre.

**4. Consider the following pairs regarding the Committees of the Constituent Assembly and their Chairmen:**
1. States Committee: Sardar Patel
2. Steering Committee: Dr. Rajendra Prasad
3. Provincial Constitution Committee: Jawaharlal Nehru
4. Rules of Procedure Committee: Dr. Rajendra Prasad
**Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?**
A) 1 and 3 only
B) 2 and 4 only
C) 2, 3, and 4 only
D) 1, 2, 3, and 4
**Answer: B.** Pair 1 is incorrect (States Committee was chaired by Nehru). Pair 3 is incorrect (Provincial Constitution Committee was chaired by Sardar Patel).

**5. On November 26, 1949, when the Constitution was adopted, which of the following provisions came into force immediately?**
1. Citizenship
2. Elections
3. Provisional Parliament
4. Fundamental Rights
**Select the correct answer:**
A) 1, 2, and 4 only
B) 1, 2, and 3 only
C) 2 and 3 only
D) 1, 2, 3, and 4
**Answer: B.** Articles related to Citizenship, Elections, Provisional Parliament, and temporary/transitional provisions (Arts 5-9, 60, 324, 366, 367, 379, 380, 388, 391, 392, 393) came into force on Nov 26, 1949. Fundamental Rights came into force on Jan 26, 1950.
topics.oneLineRevision: The making of the Indian Constitution was a collaborative process of nearly three years where a sovereign Constituent Assembly transformed the "Objectives Resolution" into a comprehensive legal document that balances idealism with administrative pragmatism.
topics.linkedTopics: Preamble, Salient Features of the Constitution, Sources of the Constitution, Union and its Territory, Citizenship.

topics.oneLineRevision: Municipalities are constitutionalized urban local bodies under Part IX-A that manage 18 functional items through direct elections and mandatory 5-year cycles to ensure grassroots urban democracy.
topics.trapZone:
* **Election Authority:** Elections to municipalities are conducted by the **State Election Commission**, NOT the Election Commission of India.
* **Voter Age:** While the voting age is 18, the minimum age to contest elections for a municipality is **21 years**.
* **Dissolution:** If a municipality is dissolved, elections must be held within 6 months. The newly elected body lasts only for the **remainder of the term**, not a fresh 5-year term.
topics.title: Municipalities
topics.coreConcept: Municipalities are self-governing units for urban areas, constitutionalized by the **74th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992**. They aim to decentralize power, ensuring that urban development and civic amenities are managed by locally elected representatives rather than a centralized state bureaucracy.
topics.teaser: High-yield revision topic for Municipalities.
topics.whyThisMatters: Municipalities represent the **third tier of governance** in India. For UPSC, this is high-yield because it intersects with Constitutional Amendments (74th), grassroots democracy, and urban planning—a major focus given India's rapid urbanization. Questions frequently appear in both Prelims (articles/powers) and Mains (functional challenges/fiscal federalism).
topics.deepUnderstanding:
The 74th Amendment added **Part IX-A** and **Articles 243-P to 243-ZG**. It provides for three types of municipalities:
1.  **Nagar Panchayat:** For areas in transition from rural to urban.
2.  **Municipal Council:** For smaller urban areas.
3.  **Municipal Corporation:** For larger urban areas.

**Key Provisions:**
* **Composition:** All members are directly elected by the people. However, the State Legislature may provide for the representation of persons with special knowledge (without voting rights in the council).
* **Reservation:** Seats are reserved for SCs and STs in proportion to their population. **One-third** of the total seats must be reserved for women (including the number of seats reserved for SC/ST women).
* **Finance Commission:** The State Finance Commission (appointed under Art 243-I for Panchayats) also reviews the financial position of municipalities and recommends the distribution of taxes between the State and Municipalities.
topics.linkedFacts:
* **District Planning Committee (Art 243-ZD):** Consolidates plans prepared by both Panchayats and Municipalities in the district.
* **Metropolitan Planning Committee (Art 243-ZE):** Created for metropolitan areas (population of 10 lakhs or more) to prepare a draft development plan.
* **Central Council of Local Government:** An advisory body established in 1954 under Art 263 of the Constitution (Presidential Order) to coordinate urban local body matters.
topics.mcqs:
**1. Consider the following statements regarding the 74th Constitutional Amendment Act:**
I. It is mandatory for every state to provide reservation for Other Backward Classes (OBCs) in municipalities.
II. The Chairperson of a municipality must be elected directly by the people of the municipal area.
III. The State Election Commissioner is appointed by the Governor but can be removed only in a manner similar to a Judge of a High Court.
**Which of the statements given above is/are correct?**
A) I and II only
B) III only
C) II and III only
D) I, II and III
**Answer: B.** OBC reservation is voluntary for states; the mode of electing the Chairperson is decided by the State Legislature (can be direct or indirect).

**2. With reference to the District Planning Committee (DPC), consider the following:**
I. Four-fifths of the members of a DPC should be elected by the elected members of the district panchayat and municipalities in the district.
II. The Constitution specifies the manner in which the chairpersons of such committees shall be chosen.
**Which of the statements given above is/are correct?**
A) I only
B) II only
C) Both I and II
D) Neither I nor II
**Answer: A.** The Constitution leaves the manner of choosing the Chairperson to the State Legislature, not specified within the Act itself.

**3. Which of the following is NOT a function listed under the 12th Schedule of the Constitution?**
A) Regulation of land-use and construction of buildings.
B) Vital statistics including registration of births and deaths.
C) Implementation of land reforms and soil conservation.
D) Urban forestry and protection of the environment.
**Answer: C.** Land reforms and soil conservation are functions of Panchayats under the 11th Schedule.

**4. If a Municipality is dissolved before the expiry of its five-year term:**
A) It must be reconstituted within 3 months.
B) The fresh elections are held for a full term of five years.
C) If the remainder period is less than six months, it is not necessary to hold elections.
D) The Administrator appointed by the Governor carries out functions for the rest of the term.
**Answer: C.** Under Art 243-U, if the remaining period is less than 6 months, an election is not mandatory to fill the vacancy for that period.

**5. Consider the following statements about Metropolitan Planning Committees (MPC):**
I. At least two-thirds of the members of an MPC should be elected by the elected members of the municipalities and chairpersons of the panchayats in the metropolitan area.
II. Every MPC shall, in preparing the draft development plan, consult such institutions and organizations as the Governor may specify.
**Which of the statements given above is/are correct?**
A) I only
B) II only
C) Both I and II
D) Neither I nor II
**Answer: C.** Both are constitutional requirements under Art 243-ZE.
topics.upscGoldPoint: The **12th Schedule** contains **18 functional items** (unlike the 29 for Panchayats). Also, while the constitution mandates the creation of Ward Committees, they are mandatory only for municipalities having a population of **3 lakhs or more**.
topics.memoryTrick:
**"U-18"**
* **U**rban Local Bodies have **18** functions.
* (Contrast this with **P-29**: **P**anchayats have **29** functions).

---
topics.prelimsSnapshot:
* **Constitutional Part:** IX-A
* **Schedule:** 12th
* **Articles:** 243-P to 243-ZG
* **Women's Reservation:** Minimum 1/3rd (Mandatory)
* **Tenure:** 5 years
* **Audit:** State Legislature provides for the manner of maintenance and auditing of accounts.
topics.linkedTopics: 73rd Amendment Act, State Finance Commission, State Election Commission, Urbanization in India, SMART Cities Mission, PESA Act.

topics.title: National Commissions for SCs, STs, and OBCs
topics.teaser: Crucial for social justice and constitutional mandates.
topics.whyThisMatters: These commissions are the primary constitutional safeguards for India’s marginalized communities. UPSC consistently tests this area because it intersects **Social Justice** and **Polity**. Understanding the bifurcation of these bodies, their quasi-judicial powers, and the specific constitutional amendments that created them is essential for both Prelims and GS Paper II (Social Justice).
topics.coreConcept: The National Commissions for Scheduled Castes (NCSC), Scheduled Tribes (NCST), and Backward Classes (NCBC) are **Constitutional Bodies** established to investigate and monitor all matters relating to the safeguards provided for these groups under the Constitution or any other law.
topics.upscGoldPoint: All three commissions possess the **powers of a Civil Court** while investigating any matter or inquiring into any complaint. This means they can summon witnesses, require the discovery and production of documents, and receive evidence on affidavits. However, they **cannot** punish perpetrators; they can only recommend action.
topics.deepUnderstanding:
Initially, a single Special Officer served all groups. The evolution is a common UPSC target:

* **Bifurcation (89th Amendment, 2003):** The erstwhile combined commission was split into the **NCSC (Article 338)** and the **NCST (Article 338A)**.
* **Constitutional Status for OBCs (102nd Amendment, 2018):** The NCBC, which was previously a statutory body, was granted constitutional status under **Article 338B**.
* **Composition:** Each commission consists of a Chairperson, a Vice-Chairperson, and three other members. They are appointed by the **President** by warrant under his hand and seal. Their conditions of service and tenure are determined by the President (usually 3 years).
* **Functions:** * Inquiry into specific complaints regarding deprivation of rights.
    * Participation and advice on the planning process of socio-economic development.
    * Submission of **Annual Reports** to the President (who lays them before Parliament). If a report relates to a state, a copy is sent to the **Governor**.
topics.linkedFacts:
* **Civil Court Status:** Granted under Articles 338(8), 338A(8), and 338B(8).
* **Nodal Ministries:** NCSC and NCBC report to the Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment; NCST reports to the **Ministry of Tribal Affairs**.
* **Mandatory Consultation:** The Union and every State Government **must consult** the commissions on all major policy matters affecting SCs, STs, and OBCs respectively.
* **Anglo-Indians:** Originally, the NCSC was also required to look after the safeguards of the Anglo-Indian community.
topics.trapZone:
* **Binding Recommendations:** A common trap—the recommendations of these commissions are **not binding** on the government. However, the government must explain the reasons for non-acceptance in a memorandum.
* **Reporting:** They submit reports to the **President**, not the Parliament directly. It is the President who causes the report to be laid before Parliament.
* **Bifurcation Year:** Don't confuse the 89th Amendment (2003) with the year it came into effect (2004).
topics.memoryTrick:
**"8-A-B" (The Article Ladder):**
* **338:** SC (The Original)
* **338-A:** ST (A for Adivasi/Added later)
* **338-B:** BC (B for Backward Classes)
topics.prelimsSnapshot:
* **NCSC:** Article 338.
* **NCST:** Article 338A (89th Amendment).
* **NCBC:** Article 338B (102nd Amendment).
* **Appointed by:** President.
* **Power:** Civil Court.
* **Reports:** Annual (to President).
topics.mcqs:
**1. Consider the following statements regarding the National Commission for Scheduled Tribes (NCST):**
1. It was established by the 65th Constitutional Amendment Act.
2. The Chairperson of the commission is given the rank of a Cabinet Minister of the Union.
3. It has the power to regulate its own procedure.
**Which of the statements given above is/are correct?**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 2 and 3 only
C) 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: B.** Statement 1 is false (it was the **89th** Amendment; the 65th created the combined commission). Statement 2 and 3 are correct constitutional/administrative facts.

**2. With reference to the National Commission for Backward Classes (NCBC), consider the following:**
1. It was originally established as a statutory body on the recommendation of the Mandal Commission.
2. The 102nd Amendment Act took away the power of the State governments to identify the Socially and Educationally Backward Classes (SEBCs).
**Which of the statements given above is/are correct?**
A) 1 only
B) 2 only
C) Both 1 and 2
D) Neither 1 nor 2
**Answer: A.** Statement 2 is incorrect because the **105th Amendment Act (2021)** restored the power of State governments to identify and maintain their own lists of SEBCs.

**3. In which of the following matters does the National Commission for Scheduled Castes (NCSC) possess the powers of a Civil Court?**
1. Summoning and enforcing the attendance of any person from any part of India.
2. Discovery and production of any document.
3. Issuing commissions for the examination of witnesses and documents.
**Select the correct answer:**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 2 and 3 only
C) 1 and 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: D.** These are the standard powers vested in the commissions under their respective articles.

**4. The report of the National Commission for Scheduled Castes is placed before the State Legislature by:**
A) The President of India.
B) The Governor of the State.
C) The Chairperson of the Commission.
D) The Speaker of the Legislative Assembly.
**Answer: B.** The Commission sends the report to the President, who forwards the relevant portion to the Governor, who then lays it before the State Legislature.

**5. Which Article of the Indian Constitution mandates that the Union and State governments shall consult the National Commission for Scheduled Castes on all major policy matters affecting them?**
A) Article 338(5)
B) Article 338(9)
C) Article 340
D) Article 342
**Answer: B.** Article 338(9) makes this consultation mandatory, ensuring the commission acts as a stakeholder in governance.
topics.oneLineRevision: The National Commissions for SCs, STs, and OBCs are President-appointed constitutional watchdogs with civil court powers, tasked with monitoring safeguards and advising on the socio-economic development of marginalized groups.
topics.linkedTopics: National Commission for Minorities, Social Justice (GS II), Article 15 & 16, Fifth and Sixth Schedules, 102nd and 105th Amendment Acts.

topics.mcqs:
**1. Consider the following statements regarding the Chairperson of the NHRC:**
1. Only a retired Chief Justice of India is eligible to be appointed as the Chairperson.
2. The Chairperson is appointed by the President on the recommendation of a committee headed by the Union Home Minister.
3. He is eligible for reappointment for a second term.
**Which of the statements given above is/are correct?**
A) 3 only
B) 1 and 3 only
C) 2 and 3 only
D) 1, 2, and 3
**Answer: A.** Statement 1 is false (post-2019, SC judges are eligible). Statement 2 is false (Committee is headed by the **Prime Minister**).

**2. The National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) can inquire into a violation of human rights only within:**
A) 6 months of the occurrence.
B) 1 year of the occurrence.
C) 2 years of the occurrence.
D) 5 years of the occurrence.
**Answer: B.** Section 36(2) of the PHRA 1993 prohibits the commission from inquiring into matters after one year.

**3. Which of the following are 'Ex-officio' members of the NHRC?**
1. Chairperson of the National Commission for Scheduled Castes.
2. Chairperson of the National Commission for Women.
3. Chief Commissioner for Persons with Disabilities.
4. Chairperson of the NITI Aayog.
**Select the correct answer:**
A) 1 and 2 only
B) 2, 3, and 4 only
C) 1, 2, and 3 only
D) 1, 2, 3, and 4
**Answer: C.** NITI Aayog Chairperson (the PM) is not a member of the commission; he heads the selection committee.

**4. With reference to the NHRC's powers regarding the Armed Forces, which of the following is correct?**
A) The Commission can investigate the violation on its own through its investigation wing.
B) The Commission can only seek a report from the Central Government and make recommendations.
C) The Commission's recommendations regarding armed forces are binding on the government.
D) The Commission has no jurisdiction whatsoever over the armed forces.
**Answer: B.** The procedure for armed forces is restricted under Section 19 of the Act.

**5. After the Chairperson or a member of the NHRC ceases to hold office, they are:**
A) Eligible for further employment under the Government of India or any State Government.
B) Ineligible for further employment under the Government of India or any State Government.
C) Eligible only for judicial appointments in the High Courts.
D) Eligible to become the Governor of a state.
**Answer: B.** This provision ensures the independence and impartiality of the members during their tenure.
topics.title: National Human Rights Commission (NHRC)
topics.prelimsSnapshot:
* **Status:** Statutory (1993).
* **Tenure:** 3 years / 70 years.
* **Selection:** 6-member committee.
* **Power:** Civil Court (investigative), but recommendations are advisory.
* **Limitation:** 1-year time bar for cases.
* **Armed Forces:** Only recommendatory role.
topics.coreConcept: The NHRC is a **Statutory Body** (not a constitutional one) established in 1993 under the **Protection of Human Rights Act (PHRA)**. It is an independent body tasked with protecting the rights relating to life, liberty, equality, and dignity of the individual as guaranteed by the Constitution or embodied in International Covenants.
topics.upscGoldPoint: The NHRC is often called a **"Toothless Tiger"** because its recommendations are **not binding** on the government. Furthermore, it has no power to punish the violators of human rights, nor to award any monetary relief to the victim on its own.
